---
title: "José Arteta"
about:
  template: trestles
  id: about-block
  image: "img/my_image.jpg"
  image-shape: round
  image-width: 13em
  links:
    - icon: envelope
      text: Email
      href: "mailto:jose.p.arteta@gmail.com"
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/josearteta
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/jose-arteta/
    - icon: twitter
      text: Twitter
      href: https://twitter.com/puentes_arteta
---

::: {#about-block}
:::

[PhD Student in Economics &nbsp;·&nbsp; Urban & Development Economics]{.home-tagline}

I am a Brazilian-Colombian economist. My research studies how public policies shape urban outcomes, with a focus on crime, mobility, and cities in the developing world. I use quasi-experimental methods and causal inference.

## Education

**Ph.D. in Economics** · 2025 – Present
University of British Columbia · *Vancouver, Canada*

**M.Sc. in Economics** · 2022 – 2024
EPGE-FGV · *Rio de Janeiro, Brazil*

**B.A. in Economics** · 2017 – 2021
Universidad de los Andes · *Bogotá, Colombia*

## Research

**No Need for Speed: Speed Limit Reductions Do Not Increase Vehicle Robberies** &nbsp;<span class="badge-wp">Working Paper</span>

I analyze the causal effect of São Paulo's 2015 speed reduction policy on vehicle robberies. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design robust to treatment heterogeneity, I find that the policy had no significant effect on crime — contradicting concerns that slower traffic exposes drivers to robbery.

[[PDF](research/papers/no-need-for-speed.pdf)] &nbsp; [[Abstract & Details](research/index.qmd)]
